headlineSummary: Anthropic and Google Gemini drew mixed-neutral reactions with a close tie, Microsoft Copilot had limited evidence for a mildly positive signal, and OpenAI skewed negative amid repeated privacy, trust, and quality complaints [E3] [E7] [E12] [E15] [E19].

ranking.summary: OpenAI drew the most negative reaction, with comments criticizing its credibility, model quality, watermarking approach, privacy practices, and ads-related trust concerns despite a neutral news item and one mild defense [E10] [E11] [E14] [E15] [E16] [E17] [E18] [E19] [E20] [E21].

Anthropic landed in mixed-neutral territory with supportive hiring and workflow comments offset by fear, replacement rhetoric criticism, and privacy concerns; its position is in a close tie with Google Gemini [E1] [E2] [E3] [E13] [E19].

Google Gemini also came out mixed-neutral, with praise for outputs and model performance balanced against criticism of pricing, benchmark results, and quota usage; it is in a close tie with Anthropic [E4] [E5] [E6] [E7] [E8] [E9].
Microsoft Copilot showed a mildly positive but limited evidence signal, driven by a comparison that said it was better for handling comments and lint errors [E12].

evidence.excerpt: I’ve joined Anthropic

annotations.rationale: The title is a straightforward joining announcement about Anthropic without explicit praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: Karpathy is talented and to me he always seemed like someone who would be against building something like skynet. Anthropic is lucky to have him.

annotations.rationale: Saying Anthropic is lucky to have Karpathy is mild praise for Anthropic's hiring.

evidence.excerpt: Anyone else fearing Anthropic more and more each day? Not from a perspective that they are doing so well, but rather that it's like an industry tornado, sucking up and destroying everything in it's path.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says they fear Anthropic and likens it to a destructive tornado.

evidence.excerpt: Gemini 3.5 Flash

annotations.rationale: The title names a Gemini model release without evaluative language.

evidence.excerpt: Interesting pricing direction. I don't think we have ever seen a 3x price increase for in the immediate next same-sized model (and lol @ 3 only ever getting a preview). 3.5 flash costs similar to Gemini 2.5 pro which was $1.25/$10

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes Gemini 3.5 Flash pricing as a sharp increase versus adjacent Gemini models.

evidence.excerpt: Gemini 3.5 Flash's 2000 token clocks aren't bad.

annotations.rationale: Saying the model's '2000 token clocks aren't bad' is mild praise of performance.

evidence.excerpt: On my Agentic SQL benchmark it scores 19/25. That's... mediocre. It means performs worse than 3.1 Flash Lite Preview (22/25), is slower (367s vs 142s) and is more expensive (75c vs 2c). It is outperformed by Gemma4 26B-A4B in every way(!)

annotations.rationale: The comment calls the model mediocre and worse, slower, and more expensive than alternatives.

evidence.excerpt: I have google ai pro plan and tried antigravity with 3.5 flash but it used up all my quota in two prompts. If that is not a bug then it is seriously unusable.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says 3.5 Flash exhausted quota almost immediately and was 'seriously unusable'.

evidence.excerpt: I asked Gemini for a video of 'pelican riding a unicycle in hyde park' - I was blown away by the output:

annotations.rationale: Being 'blown away by the output' is strong praise for Gemini's video generation quality.

evidence.excerpt: it was introduced in the initial GPT report ("Sparks of Artificial General Intelligence: Early experiments with GPT-4" [1]) by Microsoft about 3 years ago. Shortly after that it was parroted by a network of booster accounts and became a thing every clueless AI hype peddler does to "test" models. 100% marketing, 0% science.

annotations.rationale: The commenter characterizes a GPT-4-related report as '100% marketing, 0% science,' criticizing credibility around the OpenAI model.

evidence.excerpt: even now when I'm trying to 'vibe code' a game with the latest models (combination of Codex w/ gpt5.5 and gpt5.3-codex), they really do struggle. They definitely get something barebones up and running, but it's far from a fully fledged application.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Codex and GPT models 'really do struggle' and fall far short of a full application.

evidence.excerpt: Claude code does this well (responding to lint errors and comments). Cursor you have to poke the agent to look at the comments or lint errors, and even then it's about 10% good. Even GitHub Copilot is better here.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Claude Code handles this workflow well relative to Cursor.
Saying 'Even GitHub Copilot is better here' is mild praise for Copilot in this comparison.

evidence.excerpt: It will be interesting to see how the Pope's more human centered view clashes with Anthropic's rhetoric around replacing humans with AI

annotations.rationale: The comment criticizes Anthropic's rhetoric as being about replacing humans with AI.

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI Adopts Google's SynthID Watermark for AI Images with Verification Tool

annotations.rationale: The title reports OpenAI adopting a watermarking system, without clear praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: This is just performative nonsense. As someone that creates things with tools with different media I would just hard avoid this tool that adds... arbitrary metadata not of my choosing.

annotations.rationale: In the OpenAI watermark story, the commenter calls the feature 'performative nonsense' and says they would avoid the tool.

evidence.excerpt: First they verify whether a picture came from OpenAI, then they'll include subscriber data and geolocation. Well, they'll finally find out that no one wants to look at AI generated pictures or text. Once they do that, the tool will fail for the public and only work for the government.

annotations.rationale: The comment expresses distrust that OpenAI verification will expand into intrusive subscriber and geolocation tracking.

evidence.excerpt: Good. Despite people saying it will be removed, I have seen no reproducible repo demonstrating it.

annotations.rationale: Within the OpenAI watermark thread, 'Good' and the note about no demonstrated removal indicate mild support for the watermark's robustness.

evidence.excerpt: Seems inferior to C2PA, which is actually an open standard: https://contentauthenticity.org/

annotations.rationale: The commenter directly judges the OpenAI-linked approach as inferior to an alternative standard.

evidence.excerpt: I think one thing that people are sleeping on is passing a ton of secrets to OpenAI and Anthropic or your OpenRouter by having a .env or secrets on disk in your repo, but not checked in Your LLM will happily read the entire file, ship it off to be training data for future versions of ChatGPT, and not raise any flags

annotations.rationale: The commenter warns that sending secrets to OpenAI/ChatGPT could leak them into future training data, expressing strong distrust.
Anthropic is explicitly grouped with providers that the commenter says may ingest secrets from local files, a strong privacy criticism.

evidence.excerpt: They also uploaded sensitive docs in chatgpt [1]

annotations.rationale: Mentioning sensitive documents being uploaded in ChatGPT is negative privacy evidence for OpenAI.

evidence.excerpt: the reason OpenAI introducing ads bothers me is the implicit (or even explicit) bias that can be smuggled into a chat in ways that simply aren't possible when you're just clicking through to an external source.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says OpenAI introducing ads is bothersome because of conversational bias, expressing distrust.
